metadata.title: Eigenvectors: the directions a matrix can't turn

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The fan of eight arrows where six scatter and two slide along their own lines finally made eigenvectors concrete for me. I want to find those two directions by hand. Take the matrix with eigenvalues 3 and minus 1 from the example and (1) show me how solving det(A minus lambda I) equals zero produces exactly those two stretch factors, (2) find the actual eigenvector arrow for each eigenvalue, and (3) tie the negative eigenvalue's flip-and-stretch back to what a measurement outcome would read off a detector.

props.command: The fan of eight arrows where six scatter and two slide along their own lines finally made eigenvectors concrete for me. I want to find those two directions by hand. Take the matrix with eigenvalues 3 and minus 1 from the example and (1) show me how solving det(A minus lambda I) equals zero produces exactly those two stretch factors, (2) find the actual eigenvector arrow for each eigenvalue, and (3) tie the negative eigenvalue's flip-and-stretch back to what a measurement outcome would read off a detector.
props.segment: Eigenvectors: the directions a matrix can't

beats.narration_text: A matrix acts on a vector and rotates it. Every arrow in the plane points somewhere new. Almost every arrow.

card.dek: Eigenvectors: the directions a matrix can't turn

beats.narration_text: Apply a linear map to a fan of arrows and watch them scatter to new angles. But two directions come out pointing exactly where they went in. Why does every matrix have these special directions?

card.dek: Every matrix rotates most arrows — but some it can only stretch. Why must those directions exist?

beats.narration_text: Take an arbitrary matrix. Apply it to a ring of unit arrows. Watch where they land — they fan out to new directions, rotated and scaled by different amounts.
Most arrows tilt after the transformation. The question is whether any arrow stays on its own line — stretched but not rotated. That arrow would satisfy A times v equals lambda times v.
The eigenvectors are exactly those arrows. Watch the transformation: a general arrow fans to a new angle, but the eigenvectors slide only along their own line.
Each eigenvector has a corresponding eigenvalue — the stretch factor. A negative eigenvalue means the arrow flips and stretches. It still stays on its own line.
In quantum mechanics, measuring an observable projects the system into an eigenstate. The eigenvalue is the measurement outcome — the number you read off the detector.
Energy levels, quantum numbers, spin outcomes — all of quantum mechanics is built on finding the invariant directions of the right operator.

props.headline: Energy levels, quantum numbers, spin outcomes — all of quantum mechanics…

beats.narration_text: Illustrative: a two-by-two matrix with eigenvalues 3 and minus 1. Apply it to a fan of eight arrows. Six fan away from their starting angles. Two stay on their own lines — one stretched by 3, one flipped and stretched.
A matrix rotates almost every arrow. The eigenvectors are the exceptions — only stretched, never turned. Every measurement outcome in quantum mechanics is one of these special directions.

card.dek: Eigenvectors: invariant directions, only stretched. Measurement = projection onto one.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
